title: API Security
objectives: Understand API security via a real-life analogy
Know the OWASP API Security Top 10
Layer defenses: transport, auth, input validation, rate limiting, monitoring
Recognize the classic attacks (BOLA, SQL injection, mass assignment, IDOR)

sections.markdown:
**Think of API security as the layered defenses of a medieval castle.**

- **The moat + drawbridge (TLS/HTTPS)** — nobody gets close without you seeing them. Everything is over encrypted transport.
- **The gate + guards (Authentication)** — nobody enters without ID. Bearer token, JWT, mTLS.
- **The keep + inner doors (Authorization)** — even inside the castle, most rooms need different keys. Object-level ownership, role-based access, scopes.
- **The gatekeepers who count arrivals (Rate limiting)** — 500 knights trying to storm in at once? Slam the gate, ring the bell.
- **The scribes verifying documents (Input validation)** — check every letter for hidden ink and coded messages. Every input assumed hostile until proven innocent.
- **The lookouts on the walls (Monitoring + alerting)** — 20 failed logins from Belgium at 3 AM? Wake the captain.
- **Firebreaks between wings (Least privilege)** — if the kitchen catches fire, don't let it burn the armory.

**In code:** API security is LAYERED. Each layer independently prevents a class of attacks. Skip one and attackers find that gap in minutes. The good news: none of it is exotic — TLS everywhere, verify tokens, validate input, rate-limit, log, monitor. The bad news: getting any ONE of them wrong is catastrophic.

**Why this topic exists:** APIs are attacked constantly. Automated scanners hit every new endpoint within minutes of DNS propagation. OWASP maintains an **API Security Top 10** — the ten most-common serious vulnerabilities. Reading it once a year is the minimum bar for anyone building backend APIs.

**API security** is the practice of protecting an HTTP/RPC API against unauthorized access, data exfiltration, injection attacks, denial-of-service, and abuse. Combines: **transport security** (TLS), **authentication** (JWT, mTLS), **authorization** (RBAC, scopes, object-level ownership), **input validation** (schema, sanitization), **rate limiting** (per-client, per-endpoint), **secrets management** (Vault, KMS), **monitoring + alerting** (WAF, SIEM). The **OWASP API Security Top 10** is the canonical checklist.
## The OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023)

rows: `GET /orders/42` — did you check 42 belongs to THIS user? #1 most common.
Weak JWT config, missing MFA, guessable session IDs
PATCH exposes fields the user shouldn't change (isAdmin, roles)
No rate limit → single client OOMs your server
User can call `/admin/deleteAll` — endpoint level not checked
Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows
Bot buys 500 concert tickets in a second; no anti-automation
Server fetches attacker-supplied URLs → hits internal metadata endpoint
Debug mode in prod, default creds, missing headers, verbose errors
Old `/api/v1/` endpoint forgotten; still shipped without patches
Your app trusts a partner API's data blindly → injection

sections.markdown: **API1 (BOLA) is the #1 vulnerability by frequency.** Every senior interview asks about it. The fix is trivial in principle — check that the requested object belongs to the authenticated user — and every team keeps missing it. If you remember ONE thing from this topic, it's this.
## Sample program — BOLA / IDOR — the classic bug and its fix
## Mass assignment (API3) — the pattern you must avoid
## Rate limiting — the essential API4 defense
## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Trusting the client to enforce authorization.** 'The mobile app hides the delete button for non-admins' — attackers use curl. Server MUST enforce every rule.
**Mistake 2: Sequential integer IDs.** `/users/1`, `/users/2` — trivially enumerable. Use UUIDs so attackers can't guess.
**Mistake 3: No rate limiting on `/login`.** 10,000 password guesses per second — any weak password falls. Rate-limit auth endpoints hardest.
**Mistake 4: Verbose 500 errors in production.** Stack traces leak library versions, file paths, sometimes secrets. Log server-side; return `{code, message}` client-side.
**Mistake 5: CORS `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` with credentials.** Anyone can call your API with the user's cookies. Whitelist origins explicitly.
**Mistake 6: SQL injection via string concatenation.** `"SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '" + email + "'"`. Use parameterized queries / prepared statements / ORM.
**Mistake 7: Storing secrets in code or config.** GitHub bots scan public repos for AWS keys within seconds. Use environment variables, Vault, KMS, Secrets Manager.
**Mistake 8: No security headers.** No CSP, no HSTS, no X-Content-Type-Options. Every reverse proxy should add them; every Spring Boot app should verify.
## Best practices
**1. TLS everywhere.** No HTTP in production. HSTS header. Let's Encrypt / your provider — free.
**2. Every endpoint requires authentication** by default (except health check + `/login`). Opt out per endpoint, not opt in.
**3. Every object access checks ownership.** BOLA is the #1 vulnerability by frequency.
**4. Rate limit at multiple layers.** Per-IP (WAF/gateway), per-user (Spring filter), per-endpoint (annotations). Auth endpoints tightest.
**5. Input validation** via schemas (Bean Validation, JSON Schema, OpenAPI). Reject unexpected fields.
**6. Never return entities directly.** DTO at the boundary. Prevents mass assignment + accidental data leaks.
**7. Security headers.** HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy. Spring Security defaults are sensible.
**8. Structured logging + alerting.** Log auth failures, rate-limit hits, 5xx spikes. Alert on anomalies (SIEM: Splunk, Datadog, Elastic).
**9. Dependency scanning.** Trivy, Snyk, GitHub Dependabot on every PR. Old vulnerable deps kill you.
**10. Retire old API versions.** Deprecate publicly (Sunset header), then remove. Old versions accumulate CVEs.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Force TLS.** Redirect HTTP → HTTPS at the reverse proxy; add HSTS header.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Fix a BOLA.** Take an endpoint that returns `orders/{id}`; add ownership check via `@PreAuthorize`; write a test proving one user can't read another's order.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Rate limit `/login`.** Bucket4j: 5 attempts / minute / IP; return 429 with Retry-After.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — DTO layer.** Refactor any endpoint that accepts an entity to accept a DTO; write a test proving mass assignment is blocked.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Dependency + secret scanning.** Add Trivy to CI; add gitleaks to pre-commit; break the build on findings.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Penetration test your own app.** Run OWASP ZAP or Burp Suite against a staging endpoint; fix findings.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is BOLA / IDOR and how do you prevent it?**

**A:** **BOLA (Broken Object Level Authorization)** — aka IDOR (Insecure Direct Object Reference) — is the #1 API vulnerability by frequency in the OWASP Top 10. The pattern: `GET /orders/42` returns order 42 without checking whether it belongs to the authenticated user. An attacker iterates IDs and reads everyone's data. **Prevention:** every object-level endpoint MUST enforce ownership. Options: **(1)** `@PreAuthorize("@checker.canAccess(#id, authentication)")` — Spring method security with a SpEL check. **(2)** Query-level filtering — `findByIdAndUserId(orderId, currentUserId)` instead of `findById`; missing = 404 whether the order exists or not (also hides ID enumeration). **(3)** UUIDs instead of sequential integers — attackers can't guess valid IDs. Combine all three. **Test the negative case** — write a test where user A tries to fetch user B's order; assert 404 or 403.

**Q2: How do you defend against SQL injection?**

**A:** **NEVER concatenate user input into SQL.** Use **parameterized queries / prepared statements**. Every ORM (Hibernate, Doctrine, ActiveRecord) does this by default when used correctly. In JPA: `@Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email")` — the `:email` is a bound parameter, not concatenated. In raw JDBC: `PreparedStatement.setString(1, email)`. Bad: `"SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '" + email + "'"` — attacker sends `email = ' OR 1=1--` → dump all users. **Additional layers:** input validation (whitelist expected format), least-privilege DB user (app account can't `DROP TABLE`), WAF rules for common injection patterns. **Rule:** parameterized queries are the ONE non-negotiable. Every other defense is complementary.

**Q3: What is mass assignment and how do you prevent it?**

**A:** Mass assignment (OWASP API3 — Broken Object Property Level Authorization) occurs when an endpoint accepts a JSON body that copies directly onto a persistence entity. If the entity has a field like `isAdmin` or `roles`, the attacker can include those in the payload and promote themselves. **Fix:** never accept entities directly. Use a **DTO** with ONLY the fields the user is allowed to change. `UpdateProfileRequest { name, email, bio }` — no `isAdmin`, no `roles`, no `id`. Map explicitly to the entity in your service. **Rails equivalent:** `strong_parameters`. **Spring's `@JsonIgnoreProperties`** can help but is fragile — DTOs are cleaner. **Rule:** every write endpoint has a dedicated request DTO. Same principle applies to READ endpoints — never return entities; use response DTOs to control what you expose.

**Q4: Why rate-limit and how?**

**A:** Without rate limits, one client can DoS your API (accidentally or maliciously), brute-force passwords, scrape data, or exhaust your quotas with a downstream service. **How:** token-bucket algorithm — each client gets N requests per time window; excess = 429 with `Retry-After` header. **Layers:** (1) Edge/WAF — Cloudflare, AWS WAF — coarse per-IP. (2) API gateway — Kong, Envoy — per-key. (3) Application — Bucket4j in Spring, express-rate-limit in Node — per-user, per-endpoint. **Different limits per endpoint:** `/login` tightest (5/min/IP), `/list-orders` looser (100/min/user), public search moderate. **Distributed:** back the bucket with Redis so limits enforce across pods. **Response:** 429 status, `Retry-After` header, no user-identifying info in the error (prevents enumeration).

**Q5: How do you handle secrets management?**

**A:** **Never in code or config files committed to git.** GitHub bots scan public repos for AWS keys within seconds. **Instead:** **(1) Environment variables** — set via deployment platform (K8s Secrets, Vault-injected env). Local dev via `.env` files that are `.gitignore`d. **(2) Dedicated secrets managers** — HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, GCP Secret Manager, Azure Key Vault. Apps fetch at startup or via sidecar. **(3) KMS for encryption keys** — envelope encryption; DB stores encrypted data; KMS holds the master key. **(4) Rotate** — passwords, API keys, signing keys on a schedule (90 days typical). **(5) Least privilege** — each service gets its own credentials, only what it needs. **Detection:** gitleaks or truffleHog as a pre-commit hook + CI job. **Recovery:** if a secret leaks, ROTATE IMMEDIATELY, then investigate. Never 'we'll rotate next sprint'.

**Q6: What are the security headers every API should send?**

**A:** **`Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000`** (HSTS) — browser forces HTTPS. **`X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`** — prevents browsers from MIME-sniffing responses (attacker can't get JavaScript executed from an image response). **`Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'`** — locks down what scripts/frames/etc. can run (mostly for HTML responses). **`X-Frame-Options: DENY`** or **`frame-ancestors 'none'`** — prevents clickjacking. **`Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin`** — controls Referer leakage. **`Cache-Control: no-store`** — for authenticated responses so proxies don't cache PII. Spring Security applies most of these by default. **For CORS** — never `*` with credentials; explicit allowlist of origins. **Rule:** run your app through `securityheaders.com` — grade A- minimum.

**Q7: How do you approach API security end-to-end?**

**A:** Defense in depth, layer by layer: **(1) Transport** — TLS everywhere, HSTS. **(2) Auth** — every endpoint authenticated by default; short-lived tokens with proper `iss`/`aud`/`exp` verification. **(3) Authorization** — URL rules + method-level `@PreAuthorize` + object ownership checks. Never trust the client. **(4) Input validation** — Bean Validation on every DTO; reject unexpected fields. **(5) Rate limiting** — per-IP at edge, per-user at app, tight on auth endpoints. **(6) Secrets** — Vault/KMS, not code. Rotate. **(7) Monitoring** — structured logs, SIEM, alerts on auth failure spikes / 5xx bursts. **(8) Dependency scanning** — Trivy/Snyk/Dependabot on every PR. **(9) Retire old versions** — Sunset header + timeline. **(10) Regular penetration testing** — OWASP ZAP + human audit annually. **The bar:** every senior candidate should be able to walk through these ten items with an example fix for each.

## Real-world usage

- **OWASP API Security Top 10** — the canonical reference; refreshed every 3 years.
- **OWASP ZAP + Burp Suite** — daily-driver pentesting tools.
- **Snyk, Trivy, GitHub Dependabot** — dependency + container scanning.
- **HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager** — secrets in production.
- **Cloudflare, AWS WAF, Akamai** — edge rate-limiting + bot protection.
- **Splunk, Datadog, Elastic Security** — SIEM.
- **PagerDuty / Opsgenie** — alert routing when security events fire.
- **HackerOne / Bugcrowd** — bug bounty programs for public-facing APIs.

## Advanced notes

- **mTLS** — mutual TLS binds a client cert to a session; makes stolen tokens useless.
- **Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)** — mTLS between microservices with zero code changes.
- **API gateway (Kong, Envoy, Apigee)** — one place for auth + rate limit + logging.
- **WAF (Web Application Firewall)** — Cloudflare, AWS WAF — layer 7 filtering.
- **DDoS protection** — Cloudflare, AWS Shield — absorb network-layer attacks.
- **`Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only`** — CSP in observe mode before enforcing.
- **Signed URLs / capability URLs** — time-boxed access to a specific resource.
- **HMAC-signed webhooks** — verify webhook payload authenticity server-side.
- **`same-site: strict` cookies** — CSRF protection at the browser layer.
- **CVE scanning** — auto-alert when a CVE is filed against your deps.
- **SLSA levels** — supply-chain security framework for build integrity.
- **SBOM (Software Bill of Materials)** — inventory of every dep in your build.

flashcards.back: BOLA / IDOR — Broken Object Level Authorization. Always check ownership of the requested object.
Use DTOs — never accept entities directly. Only expose editable fields.
Edge/WAF (per-IP), API gateway (per-key), application (per-user + per-endpoint). Auth endpoints tightest.
Parameterized queries. NEVER concatenate user input into SQL.
Vault / KMS / Secrets Manager. NEVER in code or git.
HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options nosniff, CSP, X-Frame-Options DENY.
Transport + Auth + Authz + Input validation + Rate limit + Secrets + Monitoring + Deps + Old-version retirement + Pentesting.

challenges.text: Add HSTS + X-Content-Type-Options headers via Spring Security.
Force HTTPS at the reverse proxy.
Fix a BOLA: add ownership check to GET /orders/{id} + test.
DTO refactor: block mass assignment on PATCH /users/{id}.
Rate limit /login with Bucket4j + Redis backing for multi-pod.
Add gitleaks pre-commit + Trivy CI scan; break builds on findings.
Run OWASP ZAP against staging; fix top-3 findings; add SIEM alerts for auth-failure spikes.
